texts.orig: UNCORRECTED PROOF A R T I C L E   I N F O Article history: Received 29 January 2016 Received in revised form 11 April 2016 Accepted 13 April 2016 Available online xxx Keywords: Biogas purification Membrane contactor Absorption Dense skin membrane Intensification A B S T R A C T Gas purification technologies are a key step in the technological chain using energy sources such as natural gas, coal gas or biogas, and represent a significant part of production costs. Numerous separation technologies exist to achieve required purity specifications depending on the particular application (absorption, adsorption, cryogenic, membranes). The objective of this work is to present an evaluation of the potentialities of using a membrane contactor based on a dense skin Poly (Phenylene Oxide) (PPO) hollow fiber module for CO 2 absorption from biogas by pressurized water through simulations and experiments. In this system, the liquid flowing on the shell-side is in direct contact with the dense skin, thus enabling pressurization and avoiding membrane wetting in order to maintain stable absorption performances. The overall mass transfer coefficient K ov , CO2 removal efficiency, and CH 4 loss are evaluated for a range of liquid and gas flow rates. A one-dimensional (1D) model based on resistance in series provides very good predictions of the experimental results when the Graetz approach is used for shell-side mass transfer calculations. Membrane mass transfer resistance is shown to be negligible compared to the liquid resistance. Consequently, the overall mass transfer performances of the dense skin contactor are close to typical values of packed columns (10 -5 -10 -4 m/s). Thanks to the increased gas-liquid interfacial area offered by the membrane contactor, a significant volume reduction of the absorption unit (up to 15 process intensification factor) is potentially achievable. Moreover, a selective dense skin could minimize methane losses. © 2016 Published by Elsevier Ltd. texts.label: text
texts.orig: Biogas generation from biomass anaerobic digestion (agricultural wastes, landfill) has in recent years attracted much attention for offering a wide range of applications within a sustainable framework. Apart from being a renewable energy carrier, biogas can be burned in a combined heat and power unit (for heat and electricity generation), upgraded for natural gas pipeline injection or used as feedstock for the chemical industry [1 -4].
texts.text: Biogas generation from biomass anaerobic digestion (agricultural wastes, landfill) has in recent years attracted much attention for offering a wide range of applications within a sustainable framework. Apart from being a renewable energy carrier, biogas can be burned in a combined heat and power unit (for heat and electricity generation), upgraded for natural gas pipeline injection or used as feedstock for the chemical industry [1 -4].

texts.orig: Several processes have been proposed in order to achieve biogas purification which include absorption (in a physical or a chemical solvent), adsorption, cryogeny or membrane separation [4 -6]. Gas-liquid absorption by Pressurized Water Absorption (PWA) is currently used at industrial scale and is considered to be one of the most efficient technologies [5].

Gas-liquid absorption by Pressurized Water Absorption (PWA) is currently used at industrial scale and is considered to be one of the most efficient technologies [5].

texts.orig: The use of water as absorbent in the purification process to remove gas impurities (such as carbon dioxide, sulfur dioxide, ammonia) through absorption effectively offers a combination of major advantages: interesting thermodynamic properties (solubility, selectivity towards CH 4 ), wide availability, low cost, and an absence of solvent losses and methane contamination issues (green solvents compared to organic liquids). Moreover, PWA has been proven to be more energy efficient when compared, for example, to amine scrubbing [5] and requires a simple process design given that absorbent regeneration is achieved through simple solvent depressurization (without the need for heat exchangers or reboilers). texts.label: text
texts.orig: UNCORRECTED PROOF Membrane contactors are one of the most effective strategies for intensified gas-liquid absorption processes [7]. Large intensification factors, compared to those for packed columns, have been reported for applications such as oxygen degassing and post-combustion carbon dioxide capture by absorption in chemical solvents [8]. Similarly, carbon dioxide absorption in water by membrane contactors has been investigated by several authors (see Table A.1 in Appendix A). The various studies listed in Table A.1 are almost systematically based on microporous hydrophobic membranes. Surprisingly, no estimation of the intensification potential of membrane contactors, compared to the baseline technology (packed column) has been reported. Moreover, the use of microporous membrane addresses issues in terms of pore wetting; in that event, a large decrease of the mass transfer performances can result, leading to a significant decrease of the intensification factor, even for a slight wetting ratio of the membrane material [9]. The risk of wetting is likely to be high for biogas purification by PWA; the absorption process indeed requires high liquid pressure and a slight overpressure on the liquid side can induce massive pore wetting effects. Trace contaminants in the gas phase can also decrease the liquid surface tension, leading to increased risks of wetting. Additionally, bacteria growth in the aqueous phase can generate surface fouling and pore blocking effects [5]. One potential solution to prevent the above problems consists in using a dense, permeable membrane material in place of a microporous material. For carbon dioxide absorption in chemical solvents, this possibility has been shown to offer a remarkable wetting protection effect [10] with a negligible penalty on mass transfer performances [11]. This possibility, however, remains largely unexplored for Pressurized Water Absorption application, with the exception of two recent studies which use thick selfstanding silicone rubber membranes, of 165 and 35 µ m respectively [12,13]. In that case, the membrane mass transfer resistance is likely to be significant, thus limiting the intensification potential. It can be shown that, even with super-permeable polymers, the dense layer thickness should be limited to 5 -10 µ m in order to generate a limited mass transfer resistance for physical absorption processes [14]. texts.orig: tion and a maximal intensification potential (Fig. 2). Additionally, the mechanical resistance offered by the dense skin composite membrane opens up unique possibilities for increased energy efficiency due to the fact that the liquid solvent can be maintained under pressure during the regeneration step by gas depressurization [14]. Finally, for biogas purification purposes, the possibility with a dense polymeric layer to generate a selective mass transfer (CO 2/CH4 permeability ratio) could be of interest for limiting CH 4 losses by dissolution in the liquid solvent. This process performance variable is, in fact, important for both economical and environmental reasons [15], but is still only rarely considered in biogas purification by PWA process. To our knowledge, no study to date has reported on the use of dense skin composite hollow fiber membrane contactors for biogas purification.

texts.orig: UNCORRECTED PROOF A dense skin hollow fiber membrane module based on Poly (phenylene oxide) (PPO) has been tested for CO 2 removal from a CH4/CO2 mixture under steady-state conditions. CO 2 concentration in the feed stream of 30% under 2.5 bar has been tested corresponding to typical biogas feed conditions. Because the dense skin is located at the external side of the fibers, the liquid flows in the shell-side countercurrently to the gas phase. The overall mass transfer coefficient K ov , the CO 2 removal efficiency and the CH 4 losses are evaluated for different liquid and gas flow rates. 2.1. The module material is based on a thin dense skin of poly (phenylene oxide) (PPO) supported by a porous structure as shown in the Scanning Electron microscope images in Fig. 3. Table 2 summarizes the geometric and dense skin characteristics of the module. 2.2. texts.orig: feed gas consisting of CO2 and CH4 were supplied from the compressed gas cylinders, were adjusted individually and controlled by a mass flow meter (Brooks instrument 0154 and Brooks smart mass flow 5850S). A peristaltic pump (Watson -Marlow 520S) delivered the solvent (distilled water) from a reservoir at controlled flow rates. The inlet and outlet gas volumetric flow rates were measured using a digital gas flow meter (Definer 220 Bios International). The inlet and outlet gas phase concentrations of CO 2 and CH4 were analyzed by an infrared analyzer (MGA-3000 Multigas analyzer). A water trap and a filter were installed before the gas analyzer and the flow meter in order to remove any moisture in the outlet gas.

texts.orig: A gas mixture of CO 2 (30% vol) and CH 4 flowed on the lumen side of the membrane module (inside the fibers), counter currently to the liquid solvent flowing on the shell-side of the hollow fiber membrane contactor (HFMC). CO 2 diffused through the gas, then through the membrane and finally dissolved into water. The absorption experiments were achieved by maintaining the liquid at 22 ° C by using a refrigerated batch (ARCTIC series Refrigerated Batch Circulators-A25).

The absorption experiments were achieved by maintaining the liquid at 22 ° C by using a refrigerated batch (ARCTIC series Refrigerated Batch Circulators-A25).

texts.orig: Fig. 6 shows the effect of gas and liquid velocity on the CO 2 absorbed flux of the membrane contactor. The CO 2 absorbed flux logically significantly increases by increasing liquid velocity, as shown is Fig. 6(a). This reflects the increase of the driving force (larger relative solvent flow rate leading to a lower CO 2 concentration in the liquid) and a decrease of the boundary layer thickness (larger mass transfer coefficient in the liquid side). At constant liquid velocity, the effect of gas velocity on CO 2 absorbed flux was not significant, as shown in Fig. 6(b). This can be explained by the low resistance of the gas film. kG values indeed are estimated around 10 -1 m/s which is many orders of magnitude larger than k L values (around 10 -6 m/s).

texts.orig: One of the main interests of the dense skin membrane is its potential to offer a large mass transfer coefficient, compared to the dense self-supporting materials tested in previous studies. To that respect, the effective membrane mass transfer coefficient of the dense skin membrane tested in this study has been determined according to the Wilson plot method, which consists in plotting 1/K ov as a function of the liquid velocity rose to a negative gamma power ( u -γ ) [27]. γ is an empirical coefficient. The number with the best fit line to the experimental data will be the γ taken. The intercept of the Wilson plot with the y-axis gives the membrane mass transfer km as shown in Fig. 9. The average membrane mass transfer coefficient is around 5 · 10 -4 m/s. This value is very close to the value of 4.3.10 -4 m/s reported by Sandru et al. [16], for a similar hollow fiber module (PPO from Parker) tested in a gas permeation process. texts.orig: In a first step, a series of simulations has been performed in order to evaluate the set of operating conditions which enable the biogas target purity to be attained (y CO2,out =2%, Table 1). The same geometric and permeation properties as those of P-240 Parker module (Table 2) were considered. The process performance is evaluated for a u L /uG ratio varying from 10 to 50. The gas velocity is set at 0.1 m/s. The corresponding liquid velocity range is thus 1 -5 m/s. Under these conditions, the liquid flows in a turbulent regime (Re L >500) and the gas remains in laminar regime. The liquid mass transfer coefficient is evaluated using the correlation of Kartohardjono and Chen for shell side [26].

texts.orig: UNCORRECTED PROOF Fig. 12. Influence of interstitial liquid velocity on methane loss and biogas purity. u G =0.1 m/s, ratio u L /uG varing from 10 to 50, 22 ° C, P-240 Parker module, 1D simulations. Fig. 12 shows that biogas purity and methane loss increases as liquid velocity increases due to the increase of gas absorbed fluxes. A CO2 content lower than 2% in the outlet gas is obtained for liquid velocity above 1.5 m/s corresponding to u L /uG ratio above 15. However, the corresponding methane loss is above 12%. The interplay between biogas outlet purity and methane losses is one of the key characteristics of physical absorption processes (packed columns or membrane contactors). A 12% methane loss is clearly a strong penalty and strategies need to be explored to limit this loss rate. For physical absorption processes, solvent temperature could be one potential solution. Industrial operation of PWA for biogas purification effectively makes use of a cold temperature solvent, typically between 5 and 10 ° C [5]. Unfortunately, while a lower temperature increases CO 2 solubility and thus decreases the water flow rate for similar process performances, there is no effect on the relative CO 2/CH4 solubility (i. e. solvent selectivity), as shown in Fig. A.1. In this figure, Henry's coefficient for CO 2 and CH4 in water expressed in C G/C L (concentration in mol/m 3 ) is plotted as a function of temperature. It can be seen that the slope of the two curves is almost the same, which means that the decrease of temperature will be beneficial at the same degree for both CO 2 and CH4 absorption fluxes. Consequently, the solubility selectivity is not influenced by liquid temperature changes. Consequently, it can be expected that solvent temperature has no impact on methane losses. 4.2.2.2.. Impact of dense skin selectivity on methane losses Dense membrane contactors offer a unique opportunity to mitigate methane losses, based on their ability to offer a CO 2/CH4 membrane selectivity. To our knowledge, this potentiality has to date been unexplored. A series of simulations has thus been performed for a broad range of membrane selectivities to evaluate the impact on methane losses. Selectivities of 17 (PPO membrane), 50, 100 and 200 were tested while maintaining the same k m,CO2 value of 5.08 · 10 -4 m/s and the same module geometry. This level of CO 2/CH4 selectivity performances is far above the effective selectivity of membranes used for natural gas treatment [32,33]. The study is thus prospective in nature but Fig. A.2 shows that, based on the permeability/selectivity trade-off [34], advanced membrane materials could possibly offer this selectivity level.

texts.orig: UNCORRECTED PROOF The simulation results of the influence of membrane selectivity on methane loss and biogas purity are shown in Fig. 13 for two different u L /uG ratios: 10 (u L =1 m/s) and 50 (u L =5 m/s). It can be seen that methane loss decreases significantly as membrane selectivity increases demonstrating the interest of the selective dense layer effect. The impact is more important for higher liquid velocity values. This can be explained by the increase of k L values with liquid velocity; for large Reynolds numbers, k L becomes of the same order of magnitude than km (for u L value of 5 m/s, the predicted k L value is 2 · 10 -4 m/ s). Consequently, liquid as well as membrane resistance govern the global mass transfer and the selective role of the membrane layer becomes more pronounced. 4.2.2.3.. Process intensification potential Finally, the possibility for membrane contactors to achieve process intensification compared to packed columns has been evaluated. From the values of the product of the overall mass transfer coefficient times the specific interfacial area (K ov .a) for a given technology, the volume reduction effect can be estimated [10]. The values of K ov .a reported for the baseline technology (packed column) range between 6 × 10 -4 and 7 × 10 -2 s -1 [28]. The values of K ov .a obtained for membrane contactors from previous studies (Table A.1) or through this study (experiments and simulations) have been gathered and are compared in Table 5. Membrane contactors show in some cases mass transfer coefficients close to typical values of a conventional gas-liquid absorption process (10 -5 -10 -4 m/s). These conditions logically correspond to a negligible membrane mass transfer resistance. Thanks to the in Fig. 13. Influence of membrane selectivity on methane loss and biogas purity. u G =0.1 m/s, ratio u L /uG of 10 (for u L =1 m/s) and 50 (for u L =5 m/s), 22 ° C, P6240 Parker module geometry, 1D simulations. creased gas-liquid interfacial area offered by the hollow fiber membrane contactor, a significant intensification factor can potentially be obtained, ranging from 3 to 15. 5. Conclusion This study intended to evaluate the potentialities of dense skin membrane contactors for biogas purification by pressurized water absorption (PWA) process. Through a combined set of experimentations and simulations, the following conclusions have been obtained: i) The mass transfer performances of dense skin membrane contactors (i.e. K OV) can be comparable to the mass transfer performances of microporous membrane contactors. A wetting protection effect can thus be obtained by dense skin membranes, without a significant decrease of the mass transfer performances. ii) A 1D modeling approach, already suggested by several authors for CO 2 absorption in water by membrane contactors can be very efficient in terms of prediction, providing that the right correlation for the shell side mass transfer coefficient is taken. iii) The Graetz approach provided the best predictions of experimental data for the hollow fiber module used for this study. iv) Pressurized water absorption requires high liquid to gas velocity ratios in order to attain the product purity and methane loss specifications. Because a turbulent flow conditions are favorable for the liquid phase, liquid shell side flow is more appropriate. Gas velocities being low and under laminar flow, it is more adequate to feed the gas phase in the lumen side of the fibers. v) Similar to PWA process in packed columns, there is a trade-off between biogas purity and methane losses for membrane contactors. A lower water temperature does not appear to provide better performance in terms of methane loss (no increase of selectivity), but a lower water temperature remains interesting in terms of capacity (increased driving force). vi) An increased selectivity of the dense skin towards CO 2 , which can only be obtained by dense skin contactors, is beneficial for methane loss reduction. Very high selectivity levels (typically 50 -200) are however required for this effect to be significant. vii) Intensification factors (i.e. volume reduction) up to 15 could be attained by membrane contactors compared to packed columns, providing that the membrane mass transfer resistance is limited (thin skin membrane or non wetted high porosity microporous membrane). Several open questions remain and the following perspectives can be proposed: i) Emphasis has been placed on the mass transfer performances and the intensification effect. Given the high liquid velocities Table 5 Values of K ov and K ov .a obtained through simulations and their comparison with typical values from literature for CO 2 absorption in water using packed columns [28] and membrane contactor. The volume ratio of the membrane contactor to the packed column unit corresponds to the intensification factor.

texts.orig: UNCORRECTED PROOF ii) The removal of trace compounds (N 2 , O2, H 2 S, NH3 … ) has not been investigated. The potential interest of dense skin selectivity towards these species is another potential advantage of dense skin contactors that merits investigation. iii) The influence of operating pressure has not been investigated in this study. Increasing biogas feed pressure (typically up to 10 -15 bar) increases the driving force for CO 2 transfer allowing to reach higher absorption fluxes or to operate at lower liquid velocities. The trade-off between feed gas compression (increased operating costs) and absorption unit volume reduction (decreased capital costs) should be investigated. iv) This study focused attention exclusively on the absorption step. The interest of dense membrane contactors for water regeneration by depressurization, with the associated improved energy efficiency (liquid maintained under pressure) requires a dedicated study. Finally, more in depth analysis based on an overall and detailed parametric analysis (liquid and gas velocity, temperature, pressure, material permeation properties and module geometry) is necessary to evaluate the intensification factor compared to packed column, as well as the energy requirement of the process. These issues will be investigated in a forthcoming paper. Appendix A A.1. Henry's coefficient for CO 2 and CH4 in water as a function of temperature.
